# Innovations in Stem Cell Biology 2023

Stem cell models of development, regeneration, and disease are quickly advancing. New technologies and concepts are continuously combined with existing knowledge to create more realistic systems to improve our understanding of these intricate processes.

In this collection, we highlight papers published in 2022-2023 across Nature Portfolio journals on topics including embryonic development and stem cells, reproductive biology, synthetic tissues and embryo models, clinical and translational research and tissue stem cells.

Please review the editorial policies and peer review processes for each participating journal by visiting the links provided in the "Participating Journals" tab.

![JeWell microchips facilitate compartmentalized organoid culture and allow single-objective light sheet imaging of up to 96 organoids in 3D and in three colors in one hour.](https://media.springernature.com/w580h326/nature-cms/uploads/collections/ISSCRCollectionHero-8576888f19c5899a329c55de20a074d0.png)

## Embryoids and Organoids

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41586-022-05246-3/MediaObjects/41586_2022_5246_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Embryo model completes gastrulation to neurulation and organogenesis](/articles/s41586-022-05246-3)

Synthetic mouse embryos assembled from embryonic stem cells, trophoblast stem cells and induced extraembryonic endoderm stem cells closely recapitulate the development of wild-type and mutant natural mouse embryos up to embryonic day 8.5.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41556-022-00984-y/MediaObjects/41556_2022_984_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Stem cell-derived synthetic embryos self-assemble by exploiting cadherin codes and cortical tension](/articles/s41556-022-00984-y)

Bao et al. report that a cadherin code regulates the assembly and sorting of the first three cell lineages during mammalian development and can be manipulated to enhance the efficiency of synthetic embryogenesis.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41556-022-00929-5/MediaObjects/41556_2022_929_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Enhanced cortical neural stem cell identity through short SMAD and WNT inhibition in human cerebral organoids facilitates emergence of outer radial glial cells](/articles/s41556-022-00929-5)

Rosebrock, Arora et al. report a method to overcome limited cortical cellular diversity in human organoids, thus mirroring fundamental features of cortical development and offering a basis for organoid-based disease modelling.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41592-022-01643-8/MediaObjects/41592_2022_1643_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Geometric engineering of organoid culture for enhanced organogenesis in a dish](/articles/s41592-022-01643-8)

A scalable platform with geometrical reconfiguration of culture systems for long-term growth and maturation of organoids.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41592-022-01583-3/MediaObjects/41592_2022_1583_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [De novo construction of T cell compartment in humanized mice engrafted with iPSC-derived thymus organoids](/articles/s41592-022-01583-3)

Engraftment of human thymic organoids supports de novo development of a functional human T cell compartment in a humanized mouse model.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41592-021-01344-8/MediaObjects/41592_2021_1344_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Lineage recording in human cerebral organoids](/articles/s41592-021-01344-8)

A dual-channel recording system for high-resolution lineage tracing.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41592-022-01508-0/MediaObjects/41592_2022_1508_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Automated high-speed 3D imaging of organoid cultures with multi-scale phenotypic quantification](/articles/s41592-022-01508-0)

A method for high-content 3D imaging of organoids.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-022-33623-z/MediaObjects/41467_2022_33623_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Enhanced metanephric specification to functional proximal tubule enables toxicity screening and infectious disease modelling in kidney organoids](/articles/s41467-022-33623-z)

Proximal nephron in pluripotent stem cell derived kidney organoids are immature with limited support for functional solute channels. Vanslambrouck et al report improved metanephric specification, generating enhanced kidney organoids with superior proximal tubules, spatially arranged nephrons, and applications for disease research, and drug screening.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-022-34730-7/MediaObjects/41467_2022_34730_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Human multilineage pro-epicardium/foregut organoids support the development of an epicardium/myocardium organoid](/articles/s41467-022-34730-7)

Stem cell models of organogenesis are a valuable tool for the study of human development, but often lack the context of tissue-tissue interaction. Here they generate human multi-lineage organoids comprising pro-epicardium, septum transversum, and liver bud, which they co-culture with heart organoids to generate a physiologically relevant model of organogenesis.

## Progenitors and Progeny

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41556-023-01087-y/MediaObjects/41556_2023_1087_Figa_HTML.png)

### [Progress and challenges in stem cell biology](/articles/s41556-023-01087-y)

Since stem cells were first discovered, researchers have identified distinct stem cell populations in different organs and with various functions, converging on the unique abilities of self-renewal and differentiation toward diverse cell types. These abilities make stem cells an incredibly promising tool in therapeutics and have turned stem cell biology into a fast-evolving field. Here, stem cell biologists express their view on the most striking advances and current challenges in their field.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-022-35775-4/MediaObjects/41467_2022_35775_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Molecular versatility during pluripotency progression](/articles/s41467-022-35775-4)

During development the embryo must balance lineage specification against the preservation of plasticity using a limited molecular toolkit. In this Perspective, the authors propose Molecular Versatility as a paradigm for grouping molecular mechanisms that are repurposed through development to exert distinct functions.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41592-021-01216-1/MediaObjects/41592_2021_1216_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [A 4D single-cell protein atlas of transcription factors delineates spatiotemporal patterning during embryogenesis](/articles/s41592-021-01216-1)

A protein expression atlas of transcription factors charted onto cell lineage maps of *C**aenorhabditis* *elegans* development that uncovers mechanisms of spatiotemporal cell fate patterning and regulators of embryogenesis.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41588-022-01118-8/MediaObjects/41588_2022_1118_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Systematic identification of cell-fate regulatory programs using a single-cell atlas of mouse development](/articles/s41588-022-01118-8)

Single-cell RNA-sequencing of seven mouse developmental stages identifies lineage-specific and shared regulatory programs controlling cell-fate decisions. Cross-species analysis associates differentiation potency with ribosomal protein gene expression.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41588-023-01335-9/MediaObjects/41588_2023_1335_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Bipotent transitional liver progenitor cells contribute to liver regeneration](/articles/s41588-023-01335-9)

Transitional liver progenitor cells (TLPCs), which derive from biliary epithelial cells (BECs), differentiate into hepatocytes after serious liver damage. Notch and WNT/β-catenin signaling regulate BEC-to-TLPC and TLPC-to-hepatocyte conversions, respectively.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41588-023-01356-4/MediaObjects/41588_2023_1356_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [MLL3/MLL4 methyltransferase activities control early embryonic development and embryonic stem cell differentiation in a lineage-selective manner](/articles/s41588-023-01356-4)

Disruption of MLL3/4 enzymatic activities prevents gastrulation and leads to early embryonic lethality in mice. This is largely due to defects in extraembryonic lineages, which compromise developmental progression.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41593-022-01011-x/MediaObjects/41593_2022_1011_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Clonal relations in the mouse brain revealed by single-cell and spatial transcriptomics](/articles/s41593-022-01011-x)

Ratz et al. present an easy-to-use method to barcode progenitor cells, enabling profiling of cell phenotypes and clonal relations using single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, providing an integrated approach for understanding brain architecture.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44161-023-00215-z/MediaObjects/44161_2023_215_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Tmem88 confines ectodermal Wnt2bb signaling in pharyngeal arch artery progenitors for balancing cell cycle progression and cell fate decision](/articles/s44161-023-00215-z)

Using zebrafish as a model, Zhang et al. show that Tmem88a/b expression is required to balance proliferation and differentiation of pharyngeal arch artery progenitors into angioblasts by confining ectodermal Wnt2bb signaling.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-022-33092-4/MediaObjects/41467_2022_33092_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Murine fetal bone marrow does not support functional hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells until birth](/articles/s41467-022-33092-4)

Relatively little is known about the first hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells to arrive in the fetal bone marrow. Here they characterize the frequency, function, and molecular identity of fetal BM HSPCs and their bone marrow niche, and show that most BM HSPCs have little hematopoietic function until birth.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-022-35744-x/MediaObjects/41467_2022_35744_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [In vivo clonal tracking reveals evidence of haemangioblast and haematomesoblast contribution to yolk sac haematopoiesis](/articles/s41467-022-35744-x)

The lineage relationship between blood and endothelial cells has been difficult to examine due to the multiphasic timing of hematopoiesis in the embryo. Here the authors use using in vivo barcoding technology to assess cell ancestry and show that blood and endothelial cells emerge through common (haemangioblast) or separate (mesenchymoangioblasts and haematomesoblasts) progenitors in the yolk sac.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42003-022-04218-7/MediaObjects/42003_2022_4218_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Stem cell homeostasis regulated by hierarchy and neutral competition](/articles/s42003-022-04218-7)

A mathematical model of stem cell homeostasis is presented that comprehensively satisfies hierarchy and neutral competition is presented. The model predicts spontaneous generation of clonal bursts, which is consistent with primate hematopoietic data.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42003-023-04411-2/MediaObjects/42003_2023_4411_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Mechanical compression creates a quiescent muscle stem cell niche](/articles/s42003-023-04411-2)

Mechanical compression drives activated muscle stem cells (MuSCs) into a quiescent stem cell state providing insight into MuSC activity during injury-regeneration cycles.

## Disease Models and Therapies

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42003-022-04320-w/MediaObjects/42003_2022_4320_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Biomechanical, biophysical and biochemical modulators of cytoskeletal remodelling and emergent stem cell lineage commitment](/articles/s42003-022-04320-w)

This review highlights the biomechanical, biophysical, and biochemical modulators of cytoskeletal remodeling during tissue neogenesis in early development and postnatal healing for targeted tissue regeneration and regenerative medicine applications.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs43856-023-00254-4/MediaObjects/43856_2023_254_Figa_HTML.jpg)

### [Policy for rare diseases](/articles/s43856-023-00254-4)

Professor Bobby Gaspar is a distinguished physician-scientist who is a thought leader in translating basic research from bench-to-bedside and strategic work that facilitated bringing life-saving therapies to patients with rare diseases. He has over 30 years of experience in pediatric medicine working in the NHS and the biotechnology sector, and is the founding member of Orchard Therapeutics, where he serves as Chief Executive Officer. In this Q&A, Professor Gaspar provides insight into the regulatory approval and policy considerations for bringing novel therapies for rare diseases from discovery through to clinical application.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41588-022-01132-w/MediaObjects/41588_2022_1132_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Hijacking of transcriptional condensates by endogenous retroviruses](/articles/s41588-022-01132-w)

TRIM28 depletion in embryonic stem cells disconnects transcriptional condensates from super-enhancers, which is rescued by knockdown of endogenous retroviruses.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41593-022-01180-9/MediaObjects/41593_2022_1180_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [CRISPRi screens in human iPSC-derived astrocytes elucidate regulators of distinct inflammatory reactive states](/articles/s41593-022-01180-9)

Leng et al. establish CRISPRi screens in astrocytes to dissect pathways controlling inflammatory reactivity. They uncover two distinct inflammatory reactive signatures that are inversely regulated by STAT3 and validate that these exist in human disease.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41593-022-01131-4/MediaObjects/41593_2022_1131_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [A CRISPRi/a platform in human iPSC-derived microglia uncovers regulators of disease states](/articles/s41593-022-01131-4)

Dräger et al. establish a rapid, scalable platform for iPSC-derived microglia. CRISPRi/a screens uncover roles of disease-associated genes in phagocytosis, and regulators of disease-relevant microglial states that can be targeted pharmacologically.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41591-023-02273-z/MediaObjects/41591_2023_2273_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Motixafortide and G-CSF to mobilize hematopoietic stem cells for autologous transplantation in multiple myeloma: a randomized phase 3 trial](/articles/s41591-023-02273-z)

The phase 3 GENESIS trial reports the superiority of the novel CXCR4 inhibitor motixafortide with G-CSF in mobilizing hematopoietic progenitor cells for autologous stem cell transplantation in multiple myeloma.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41591-022-02097-3/MediaObjects/41591_2022_2097_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Neural stem cell transplantation in patients with progressive multiple sclerosis: an open-label, phase 1 study](/articles/s41591-022-02097-3)

Phase 1 trial results reveal that intrathecal transplantation of human fetal neural precursor cells in patients with progressive multiple sclerosis is feasible, safe and tolerable.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41591-022-01956-3/MediaObjects/41591_2022_1956_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Transplantation of human neural progenitor cells secreting GDNF into the spinal cord of patients with ALS: a phase 1/2a trial](/articles/s41591-022-01956-3)

A phase 1/2a study shows that human neural progenitor cells modified to release the growth factor GDNF are safely transplanted into the spinal cord of patients with ALS, with cell survival and GDNF production for over 3 years.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-023-36133-8/MediaObjects/41467_2023_36133_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Hydrogel oxygen reservoirs increase functional integration of neural stem cell grafts by meeting metabolic demands](/articles/s41467-023-36133-8)

Injectable biomimetic hydrogels hold significant promise for tissue engineering applications. Here, the authors present a hybrid myoglobin:peptide hydrogel to overcome a critical oxygen shortage following neural stem cell transplantation, thus increasing cell survival and integration.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-022-35183-8/MediaObjects/41467_2022_35183_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Bead-jet printing enabled sparse mesenchymal stem cell patterning augments skeletal muscle and hair follicle regeneration](/articles/s41467-022-35183-8)

Current mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) transplantation practices are limited by the loss or reduced performance of MSCs. Here the authors develop a bead-jet printer for intraoperative formulation and printing of MSCs-laden Matrigel beads to improve skeletal muscle and hair follicle regeneration.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-022-32868-y/MediaObjects/41467_2022_32868_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Msx1+ stem cells recruited by bioactive tissue engineering graft for bone regeneration](/articles/s41467-022-32868-y)

Critical-sized bone defects still present clinical challenges. Here the authors show that transplantation of neurotrophic supplement-incorporated hydrogel grafts promote full-thickness regeneration of the calvarium and perform scRNA-seq to reveal contributing stem/progenitor cells, notably a resident Msx1+ skeletal stem cell population.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-022-30243-5/MediaObjects/41467_2022_30243_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [An instantly fixable and self-adaptive scaffold for skull regeneration by autologous stem cell recruitment and angiogenesis](/articles/s41467-022-30243-5)

Limited stem cells and mismatched interface fusion have plagued biomaterial-mediated cranial reconstruction. Here, the authors engineer an instantly fixable and self-adaptive scaffold to promote calcium chelation and interface integration, regulate macrophage M2 polarization, and recruit endogenous stem cells.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42003-022-03402-z/MediaObjects/42003_2022_3402_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Infiltrating natural killer cells bind, lyse and increase chemotherapy efficacy in glioblastoma stem-like tumorospheres](/articles/s42003-022-03402-z)

“Super-charged” NK cells kill patient-derived glioblastoma stem-like cells (GSLCs) in 2D and 3D tumor models, secrete IFN-γ and upregulate the surface expression of CD54 and MHC class I in GSLCs.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs43856-023-00286-w/MediaObjects/43856_2023_286_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [PAM-flexible Cas9-mediated base editing of a hemophilia B mutation in induced pluripotent stem cells](/articles/s43856-023-00286-w)

Hiramoto et al. develop a base-editing approach using SpCas9-NG, an engineered Cas9 with broad PAM flexibility, to correct a causative mutation in hemophilia B. Their approach is used to repair the point mutation in patient-derived iPSCs and restore coagulation factor IX expression in HEK293 cells and knock-in mice.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs43856-022-00223-3/MediaObjects/43856_2022_223_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Intraglandular mesenchymal stem cell treatment induces changes in the salivary proteome of irradiated patients](/articles/s43856-022-00223-3)

Lynggaard et al. profile the salivary proteome and metaproteome in patients with head and neck cancer who have received radiation therapy and an intraglandular mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) treatment for radiation-induced xerostomia and in healthy controls. MSC therapy impacts the composition of the salivary proteome in the longer-term.

## Development and Reproduction

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41586-023-05834-x/MediaObjects/41586_2023_5834_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Generation of functional oocytes from male mice in vitro](/articles/s41586-023-05834-x)

Mouse induced pluripotent stem cells derived from differentiated fibroblasts could be converted from male (XY) to female (XX), resulting in cells that could form oocytes and give rise to offspring after fertilization.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41586-022-05574-4/MediaObjects/41586_2022_5574_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Metabolic regulation of species-specific developmental rates](/articles/s41586-022-05574-4)

An in vitro system that recapitulates temporal characteristics of embryonic development demonstrates that the different rates of mouse and human embryonic development stem from differences in metabolic rates and—further downstream—the global rate of protein synthesis.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41556-022-00916-w/MediaObjects/41556_2022_916_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Polycomb repressive complex 2 shields naïve human pluripotent cells from trophectoderm differentiation](/articles/s41556-022-00916-w)

Two side-by-side papers report that H3K27me3 deposited by polycomb repressive complex 2 represents an epigenetic barrier that restricts naïve human pluripotent cell differentiation into alternative lineages including trophoblasts.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41556-022-00932-w/MediaObjects/41556_2022_932_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Integrated multi-omics reveal polycomb repressive complex 2 restricts human trophoblast induction](/articles/s41556-022-00932-w)

Two side-by-side papers report that H3K27me3 deposited by polycomb repressive complex 2 represents an epigenetic barrier that restricts naive human pluripotent cell differentiation into alternative lineages including trophoblasts.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41556-023-01108-w/MediaObjects/41556_2023_1108_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [A single-cell transcriptome atlas profiles early organogenesis in human embryos](/articles/s41556-023-01108-w)

Xu et al. provide a single-cell transcriptomic atlas of 4–6 week human embryos, thereby profiling early human organogenesis.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41588-022-01147-3/MediaObjects/41588_2022_1147_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Substantial somatic genomic variation and selection for *BCOR* mutations in human induced pluripotent stem cells](/articles/s41588-022-01147-3)

Sequencing of human induced pluripotent stem cell lines highlights pervasive mutagenesis, heterogeneity between clones derived from the same individual during a single reprogramming experiment and positive selection for acquired mutations in *BCOR*.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44161-022-00065-1/MediaObjects/44161_2022_65_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Pseudodynamic analysis of heart tube formation in the mouse reveals strong regional variability and early left–right asymmetry](/articles/s44161-022-00065-1)

Using high-resolution confocal images and computational surface mapping, Esteban et al. provide a detailed pseudodynamic atlas of early heart tube development (E7.5–E8.5), develop a morphometric staging system based on landmark curves and distances in the surface of the tissues and identify parameters that can be used for precise embryo staging across different labs. This morphometric analysis reveals early signs of left–right asymmetry, before the cardiac looping stage, which is regulated by the Nodal signaling pathway.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44161-022-00124-7/MediaObjects/44161_2022_124_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Human-gained heart enhancers are associated with species-specific cardiac attributes](/articles/s44161-022-00124-7)

Destici, Zhu, et al. identify human-specific *cis*-regulatory elements (CREs) through a comparative epigenomic analysis of human and mouse cardiomyocytes at early stage of development and show that these CREs could contribute to species-specific cardiac features. Human-specific enhancers were particularly enriched in SNPs associated with human-specific traits (such as increased heart resting rate, atrial fibrillation and QRS duration), and the acquisition of human-specific enhancers could expand the functionality of the conserved transcriptional regulator ZIC3 by modifying its spatio-temporal expression.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44161-022-00183-w/MediaObjects/44161_2022_183_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [A single-cell comparison of adult and fetal human epicardium defines the age-associated changes in epicardial activity](/articles/s44161-022-00183-w)

Knight-Schrijver et al. use single-cell and single-nuclei RNA sequencing to profile the human fetal and adult epicardium in homeostatic conditions. The analysis shows fetal-specific epicardial gene programs that could support heart regeneration.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-022-31216-4/MediaObjects/41467_2022_31216_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Healthy cloned offspring derived from freeze-dried somatic cells](/articles/s41467-022-31216-4)

The development of safe preservation methods for genetic resources is important. Here, the authors successfully produce cloned mice from freeze-dried somatic cells, demonstrating the possibility of safe and low-cost preservation of genetic resources.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-022-34800-w/MediaObjects/41467_2022_34800_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Primate-specific transposable elements shape transcriptional networks during human development](/articles/s41467-022-34800-w)

The human genome harbors more than 4.5 million transposable element (TE)-derived insertions, the result of recurrent waves of invasion and internal propagation. Here they show that TEs belonging to evolutionarily recent subfamilies go on to regulate later stages of human embryonic development, notably conditioning the expression of genes involved in gastrulation and early organogenesis.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w290h158/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42003-022-03964-y/MediaObjects/42003_2022_3964_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Induced pluripotent stem cells of endangered avian species](/articles/s42003-022-03964-y)

iPSCs from three endangered avian species (including Okinawa rail, Japanese ptarmigan, and Blakiston’s fish owl) are developed and characterized as a potential resource for their conservation.

## **Stem Cell-Based Therapies for Regenerative Medicine**

Stem cell-based therapies hold great promise for the treatment of various diseases. For instance, stem cells have been used to repair damaged tissues and organs, such as the kidneys, and to promote tissue regeneration.

### Translational research

Translational research bridges the gap between laboratory findings and clinical applications and is essential in stem cell research. Scientists rely on animal models to test the safety and efficacy of new stem cell-based treatments before moving on to clinical trials involving human subjects. These trials are crucial for determining the therapeutic potential of stem cell therapies and ensuring their safety for patients.

â

## Types of Human Stem Cells

There are three main types of stem cells: embryonic stem cells, adult stem cells, and induced pluripotent stem cells.

â

### Embryonic Stem Cells

Embryonic stem cells are derived from embryos in their early developmental stages. These cells are pluripotent, meaning they can give rise to almost any cell type in the body. However, their use has been controversial due to the ethical implications of obtaining them from human embryos. This has led to the development of alternative sources of pluripotent stem cells.

â

### Adult Stem Cells

Adult stem cells, or somatic stem cells, are found throughout the body in various tissues. They are responsible for maintaining and repairing the tissue in which they reside. Unlike embryonic stem cells, adult stem cells are multipotent, meaning they can only differentiate into a limited number of cell types. However, their use does not raise the same ethical concerns as embryonic stem cells.

â

### Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells

Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) are adult cells that have been genetically reprogrammed to resemble embryonic stem cells. This breakthrough in stem cell research allows scientists to create pluripotent stem cells without the ethical concerns associated with embryonic stem cells.

iPSCs can potentially revolutionize regenerative medicine and other applications in stem cell research.

â

## Potential Applications

Stem cell research has many potential applications, including regenerative medicine, cancer, disease modeling, drug development, and tissue engineering.

â

### Regenerative Medicine

One of the most promising applications of stem cell research is in regenerative medicine, where stem cells can replace damaged or lost cells in the body. This process can potentially treat various diseases and conditions, including [Parkinson's](https://www.dvcstem.com/post/stem-cell-therapy-for-parkinsons), [multiple sclerosis](https://www.dvcstem.com/post/stem-cell-therapy-for-ms), [stroke](https://www.dvcstem.com/post/stem-cell-therapy-stroke), and [spinal cord injuries](https://www.dvcstem.com/post/stem-cell-therapy).

â

### Disease Modeling and Drug Development

Stem cells can be used to create disease models, invaluable tools for understanding the mechanisms underlying various diseases and developing new treatments. For example, induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) can be generated from patients with specific diseases, allowing researchers to study the cellular and molecular processes involved in these conditions.

This approach can lead to the discovery of new drug targets and the development of more effective therapies. Examples of diseases that can be modeled using stem cells include [Alzheimer's disease](https://www.dvcstem.com/post/stem-cell-therapy-for-alzheimers), [diabetes](https://www.dvcstem.com/post/can-stem-cells-treat-diabetes), and [kidney disease](https://www.dvcstem.com/post/can-stem-cells-help-kidney-disease).

â

### Tissue Engineering

Stem cells have the potential to revolutionize tissue engineering by providing a source of cells for the creation of functional, three-dimensional tissues and organs.

This could eventually lead to the development of lab-grown organs that can be used for transplantation, reducing the reliance on organ donors and improving the outcomes for patients needing transplants. Examples of tissues that can be engineered using stem cells include heart muscle, blood vessels, heart cells, and cartilage.

## Ethical Concerns and Controversy

Stem cell research has been a subject of ethical debate and controversy, particularly in embryonic stem cell research.

â

### Why is Embryonic Stem Cell Research Controversial?

The controversy surrounding human embryonic stem cells and cell research primarily stems from the fact that obtaining these cells requires the destruction of human embryos. This has led to ethical concerns and opposition from various religious and pro-life groups.

In response to these concerns, many countries have implemented strict regulations on using human embryonic stem cells, and some have even banned their use entirely.

â

### Adult Stem Cell Research

Adult stems cell research is an exciting and rapidly evolving field that holds great promise for the future of regenerative medicine. These multipotent cells in various tissues throughout the human body can differentiate into specialized cell types, making them valuable resources for developing innovative treatments. This section will highlight adult stem cell research's key advantages and therapeutic potential, emphasizing its positive impact on medical advancements.

â

#### Advantages of Adult Stem Cells

Adult stem cells offer several benefits over other stem cell types, such as embryonic stem cells. First, adult stem cells can be obtained from the patient's body, or unbilical cord tissue; reducing the risk of immune rejection and eliminating ethical concerns associated with embryonic stem cell research. Additionally, adult stem cells have been studied extensively, resulting in a wealth of knowledge on their biology and potential applications.

## Future Directions of Stem Cell Research: Promising Developments and Applications

Stem cell research has made significant strides in recent years, with numerous advancements shaping the future of regenerative medicine. This section explores the promising future directions of stem cell research, highlighting the potential applications and benefits in a positive light.

### Bone Marrow Transplants and Blood Cells

Stem cell research has contributed to refining and expanding bone marrow transplant techniques. These transplants, which involve the transfer of hematopoietic stem cells, have become a gold standard treatment for various blood disorders and malignancies. As research progresses, it is anticipated that the efficiency and safety of bone marrow transplants will further improve, enabling more patients to benefit from this life-saving procedure.

â

### Cardiac Tissue Engineering and Damaged Hearts

Cardiac tissue engineering, which involves generating heart-like cells from stem cells, has shown great potential in repairing damaged hearts. Researchers develop functional cardiac tissues to provide viable treatment options for heart failure patients. National institutes and research centers are investing in this area, foreseeing the transformative impact it may have on cardiovascular medicine.

â

### Liver Cells and Regenerative Medicine

Stem cell research has also uncovered the potential for regenerating liver cells, which could revolutionize the treatment of liver diseases. With further advancements, human stem cells and cell-based therapies may one day replace the need for liver transplants in certain conditions, alleviating the burden on organ transplant waiting lists and improving patient outcomes.

### Treating Disease with Novel Approaches

The potential to treat disease using stem cells extends beyond the applications above. Topics covered in recent research include using pluripotent stem cells to generate various cell types, manipulating genes to promote cellular regeneration, and developing advanced animal models for testing novel therapies.

To foster the growth of stem cell research, many national institutes and organizations are embracing open-access policies, ensuring that valuable research findings are widely available to the scientific community. ## Stem Cell Therapy: Potential Uses and Therapeutic Benefits

Stem cell therapy, a promising aspect of regenerative medicine, has garnered significant attention due to its potential to revolutionize the treatment of various diseases and conditions. By harnessing the regenerative properties of stem cells, researchers aim to develop innovative therapies that can repair or replace damaged tissues and organs. This section will discuss the potential uses of stem cell therapy in a positive light, highlighting its therapeutic benefits and the promise it holds for the future of medicine.

â

### Neurodegenerative Diseases

Stem cell therapy has great potential in addressing neurodegenerative diseases like Parkinson's, Alzheimer's, and multiple sclerosis. By replacing lost or damaged neurons and promoting the regeneration of brain cells, stem cell therapies may significantly improve the quality of life for individuals living with these debilitating conditions. Moreover, the use of pluripotent stem cells in research has facilitated a better understanding of the underlying mechanisms behind these diseases, further advancing therapeutic development.

â

### Cardiac Tissue Regeneration

Heart disease is a leading cause of mortality worldwide, and stem cell therapy offers a promising approach to repairing damaged heart muscle cells and restoring cardiac function. Stem cells, such as mesenchymal stem cells and cardiac progenitor cells, have the potential to differentiate into various cell types, including new heart cells and the heart muscle cells themselves. By promoting heart tissue regeneration, stem cell therapy may significantly improve outcomes for patients with heart failure and other cardiac conditions.

â

### Spinal Cord Injuries

Spinal cord injuries can have devastating consequences, leading to permanent paralysis and loss of function. Stem cell therapy holds promise in treating spinal cord and brain injuries by promoting the regeneration of nerve cells and repairing damaged neural pathways cells. Preclinical studies and early-phase clinical trials have shown encouraging results, with some patients experiencing improved motor function and sensory perception following stem cell treatment.

â

### Autoimmune Diseases

Autoimmune diseases, such as rheumatoid arthritis and lupus, result from the immune system attacking the body's tissues. Stem cell therapy, Â has shown potential in resetting the immune system and alleviating the symptoms of autoimmune diseases. By replacing the patient's dysfunctional immune system with healthy stem cells, stem cell treatments may offer long-term remission and improved quality of life for individuals suffering from these chronic conditions.

â

### Diabetes

Stem cell therapy may revolutionize the treatment of diabetes by replacing damaged pancreatic beta cells and restoring insulin production. Researchers are exploring using pluripotent stem cells to generate functional beta cells in the lab to transplant these cells into patients with type 1 diabetes. This approach could eliminate the need for insulin injections and improve blood sugar control, significantly enhancing the lives of those affected by this disease.

In summary, stem cell science and therapy hold immense potential for treating various diseases and conditions. With ongoing research and clinical trials, the promise of stem cell science and treatment continues to grow, offering hope for a brighter future in medicine. By focusing on the positive aspects of this innovative field, we can appreciate the therapeutic benefits and transformative potential of stem cell research and therapy.

## What is stem cell research?

Stem cell research studies undifferentiated stem cells that can develop into many different cells and new tissue types. This research aims to understand how stem cells function, how they can be used to treat diseases, and how they can be harnessed for regenerative medicine and tissue engineering applications.

Stem cell research is controversial primarily due to ethical concerns surrounding the use of human embryos in embryonic stem cell research. The destruction of embryos to obtain these cells has led to opposition from various religious and pro-life groups. Adult stem cell research raises ethical concerns, such as the potential exploitation of vulnerable populations and the safety and efficacy of specific cancer stem cells as cell therapies.

â

Ethical concerns surrounding stem cell research include the destruction of human embryos in embryonic stem cell research, the potential exploitation of vulnerable populations as sources of stem cells, and the safety and efficacy of specific stem cell therapies.

Stem cell research is funded through government grants, private funding, and donations from individuals and organizations. In some countries, government funding for stem cell research, particularly embryonic stem cell research, has been limited due to ethical concerns.

## Abstract

The induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC) technology has transformed in vitro research and holds great promise to advance regenerative medicine. iPSCs have the capacity for an almost unlimited expansion, are amenable to genetic engineering, and can be differentiated into most somatic cell types. iPSCs have been widely applied to model human development and diseases, perform drug screening, and develop cell therapies. In this review, we outline key developments in the iPSC field and highlight the immense versatility of the iPSC technology for in vitro modeling and therapeutic applications. We begin by discussing the pivotal discoveries that revealed the potential of a somatic cell nucleus for reprogramming and led to successful generation of iPSCs. We consider the molecular mechanisms and dynamics of somatic cell reprogramming as well as the numerous methods available to induce pluripotency. Subsequently, we discuss various iPSC-based cellular models, from mono-cultures of a single cell type to complex three-dimensional organoids, and how these models can be applied to elucidate the mechanisms of human development and diseases. We use examples of neurological disorders, coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19), and cancer to highlight the diversity of disease-specific phenotypes that can be modeled using iPSC-derived cells. We also consider how iPSC-derived cellular models can be used in high-throughput drug screening and drug toxicity studies. Finally, we discuss the process of developing autologous and allogeneic iPSC-based cell therapies and their potential to alleviate human diseases.

## Introduction

The development of induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC) technology has opened vast opportunities for in vitro modeling of human biology and for cell therapy applications.[1](#ref-CR1 "Rowe, R. G. & Daley, G. Q. Induced pluripotent stem cells in disease modelling and drug discovery. Nat. Rev. Genet. 20, 377–388 (2019)."),[2](#ref-CR2 "Shi, Y., Inoue, H., Wu, J. C. & Yamanaka, S. Induced pluripotent stem cell technology: a decade of progress. Nat. Rev. Drug Discov. 16, 115–130 (2017)."),[3](#ref-CR3 "Takahashi, K. et al. Induction of pluripotent stem cells from adult human fibroblasts by defined factors. Cell 131, 861–872 (2007)."),[4](#ref-CR4 "Takahashi, K. & Yamanaka, S. Induction of pluripotent stem cells from mouse embryonic and adult fibroblast cultures by defined factors. Cell 126, 663–676 (2006)."),[5](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR5 "Yu, J. et al. Induced pluripotent stem cell lines derived from human somatic cells. Science 318, 1917–1920 (2007).") Since the first reports of somatic cell reprogramming into mouse and human iPSCs in 2006 and 2007, respectively, iPSCs have been applied to model human development and diseases in vitro, screen drug candidates, and create cell therapies.[1](#ref-CR1 "Rowe, R. G. & Daley, G. Q. Induced pluripotent stem cells in disease modelling and drug discovery. Nat. Rev. Genet. 20, 377–388 (2019)."),[2](#ref-CR2 "Shi, Y., Inoue, H., Wu, J. C. & Yamanaka, S. Induced pluripotent stem cell technology: a decade of progress. Nat. Rev. Drug Discov. 16, 115–130 (2017)."),[3](#ref-CR3 "Takahashi, K. et al. Induction of pluripotent stem cells from adult human fibroblasts by defined factors. Cell 131, 861–872 (2007)."),[4](#ref-CR4 "Takahashi, K. & Yamanaka, S. Induction of pluripotent stem cells from mouse embryonic and adult fibroblast cultures by defined factors. Cell 126, 663–676 (2006)."),[5](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR5 "Yu, J. et al. Induced pluripotent stem cell lines derived from human somatic cells. Science 318, 1917–1920 (2007).") Increasing understanding of the mechanisms that govern iPSC induction has shed light on cell fate decisions, accelerating the development of efficient iPSC derivation methods and protocols for iPSC differentiation into somatic cells.[6](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR6 "Takahashi, K. & Yamanaka, S. A decade of transcription factor-mediated reprogramming to pluripotency. Nat. Rev. Mol. Cell Biol. 17, 183–193 (2016).") Modeling human biology with iPSCs and iPSC-derived cells is particularly attractive, given the human origin of iPSCs and the ability to derive patient-specific iPSCs with a disease-relevant genetic background.[2](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR2 "Shi, Y., Inoue, H., Wu, J. C. & Yamanaka, S. Induced pluripotent stem cell technology: a decade of progress. Nat. Rev. Drug Discov. 16, 115–130 (2017).") Indeed, iPSC-based cellular models may reveal human-specific phenotypes and molecular mechanisms that do not necessarily manifest in animal models.[7](#ref-CR7 "Breschi, A., Gingeras, T. R. & Guigo, R. Comparative transcriptomics in human and mouse. Nat. Rev. Genet. 18, 425–440 (2017)."),[8](#ref-CR8 "Gharib, W. H. & Robinson-Rechavi, M. When orthologs diverge between human and mouse. Brief. Bioinform. 12, 436–441 (2011)."),[9](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR9 "Lynch, V. J. Use with caution: developmental systems divergence and potential pitfalls of animal models. Yale J. Biol. Med. 82, 53–66 (2009).") Furthermore, ever increasing complexity of iPSC-based cellular models has resulted in the development of sophisticated human-like tissues, such as organoids, that contain multiple cell types, exhibit primitive human tissue-like architecture and enable modeling of higher order cell-cell interactions.[10](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR10 "Takebe, T. & Wells, J. M. Organoids by design. Science 364, 956–959 (2019).") Various iPSC-derived cellular models can be applied to probe disease mechanisms, evaluate drug activity and toxicity, and develop next-generation cell therapies. Given that iPSCs can be genetically modified and differentiated into otherwise inaccessible cell types, autologous and allogeneic cell therapies are being actively developed using the iPSC technology and hold a great promise to provide new approaches for treating complex diseases.[11](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR11 "Yamanaka, S. Pluripotent Stem Cell-based Cell Therapy- Promise And Challenges. Cell Stem Cell 27, 523–531 (2020).")

In this review, we begin by outlining the historical development of the iPSC technology, including the key discoveries that led to the breakthrough of somatic cell reprogramming to iPSCs in 2006 and 2007.[3](#ref-CR3 "Takahashi, K. et al. Induction of pluripotent stem cells from adult human fibroblasts by defined factors. Cell 131, 861–872 (2007)."),[4](#ref-CR4 "Takahashi, K. & Yamanaka, S. Induction of pluripotent stem cells from mouse embryonic and adult fibroblast cultures by defined factors. Cell 126, 663–676 (2006)."),[5](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR5 "Yu, J. et al. Induced pluripotent stem cell lines derived from human somatic cells. Science 318, 1917–1920 (2007).") Subsequently, we summarize the key molecular and cellular events governing iPSC induction as well as the methods for somatic cell reprogramming to iPSCs. We then discuss the versatile applications of iPSCs, including in vitro modeling of human development and diseases, drug discovery, and cell therapy applications.

## Historical overview of somatic cell reprogramming to iPSCs

Today, it is well established that most somatic cells harbor complete genetic information required for the development of an entire organism, whereas phenotypic diversity is achieved by epigenetic mechanisms that define gene expression potential in each cell.[12](#ref-CR12 "Gurdon, J. B. The generation of diversity and pattern in animal development. Cell 68, 185–199 (1992)."),[13](#ref-CR13 "Kiefer, J. C. Epigenetics in development. Dev. Dyn. 236, 1144–1156 (2007)."),[14](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR14 "Tompkins, J. D. Discovering DNA methylation, the history and future of the writing on DNA. J. Hist. Biol. 55, 865–887 (2022).") However, prior to such modern understanding of animal development, various hypotheses to explain how immense physiological complexity of an adult animal could emerge were contemplated. Popular in the 17th and 18th centuries, a theory of preformationism posited that animals would grow from miniature versions of themselves; the imagined homunculi were microscopic preformed human beings that would simply grow into their adult versions.[15](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR15 "Roe, S. A. Matter, life, and generation: eighteen-century embryology and the Haller-Wolff Debate, (Cambridge University Press, 1981).") As pioneering work in embryology accumulated and microscopy power improved, preformationism was gradually replaced by the theory of epigenesis, postulating sequential cell differentiation and organ development from an egg.[16](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR16 "Kilgour, F. G. William Harvey and his contributions. Circulation 23, 286–296 (1961)."),[17](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR17 "Aulie, R. P. Caspar Friedrich Wolff and his ‘Theoria generationis’, 1759. J. Hist. Med. Allied Sci. 16, 124–144 (1961).") Yet, it remained unclear how an egg cell could give rise to the breathtaking phenotypic diversity of somatic cells.

In 1892, the German evolutionary biologist August Weismann (1834–1914) proposed the germ plasm theory, also known as the Weismann barrier, postulating that germ cells alone were used to transmit heritable information, whereas acquisition of somatic cell fate involved irreversible modification of heritable information, enabling phenotypic diversity to emerge.[18](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR18 "Weismann, A. Das Keimplasma; eine Theorie der Vererbung, (Jena, Fischer, 1892).") The idea of irreversible restriction of a differentiated somatic cell state during development was reiterated by the British developmental biologist Conrad Waddington (1905–1975) in 1957.[19](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR19 "Waddington, C. H. The Strategy of the Genes; A Discussion of Some Aspects of Theoretical Biology, (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1957).") Waddington proposed a model that would become known as the Waddington’s epigenetic landscape, suggesting that cell differentiation resembled a ball rolling downhill towards a more and more restricted and irreversible state.[19](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR19 "Waddington, C. H. The Strategy of the Genes; A Discussion of Some Aspects of Theoretical Biology, (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1957).") However, it remained elusive whether somatic cell differentiation truly required irreversible mutational events to occur or whether it could be achieved by some other means, such as by reversible epigenetic mechanisms.[14](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR14 "Tompkins, J. D. Discovering DNA methylation, the history and future of the writing on DNA. J. Hist. Biol. 55, 865–887 (2022).") A year later, the American geneticist David Nanney (1925–2016) proposed that while the DNA sequence conferred gene expression potential, phenotypic differences in cells sharing the same genome could arise because of gene expression “specificities” regulated by epigenetic systems.[20](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR20 "Nanney, D. L. Epigenetic control systems. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 44, 712–717 (1958).") Indeed, the reversibility of the mechanisms governing somatic cell specification was demonstrated by the British developmental biologist John Gurdon (b. 1933), who performed somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT) experiments (Fig. [1a, b](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#Fig1)).[21](#ref-CR21 "Gurdon, J. B. The developmental capacity of nuclei taken from intestinal epithelium cells of feeding tadpoles. J. Embryol. Exp. Morphol. 10, 622–640 (1962)."),[22](#ref-CR22 "Gurdon, J. B. The transplantation of nuclei between two species of Xenopus. Dev. Biol. 5, 68–83 (1962)."),[23](#ref-CR23 "Gurdon, J. B. Adult frogs derived from the nuclei of single somatic cells. Dev. Biol. 4, 256–273 (1962)."),[24](#ref-CR24 "Gurdon, J. B. Multiple genetically identical frogs. J. Hered. 53, 5–9 (1962)."),[25](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR25 "Gurdon, J. B., Elsdale, T. R. & Fischberg, M. Sexually mature individuals of Xenopus laevis from the transplantation of single somatic nuclei. Nature 182, 64–65 (1958).") In 1962, using a model of the *Xenopus laevis* frog, Gurdon demonstrated that a nucleus isolated from a terminally differentiated somatic cell and transplanted into an enucleated egg harbored all the genetic information required to give rise to germline-competent organisms.[21](#ref-CR21 "Gurdon, J. B. The developmental capacity of nuclei taken from intestinal epithelium cells of feeding tadpoles. J. Embryol. Exp. Morphol. 10, 622–640 (1962)."),[22](#ref-CR22 "Gurdon, J. B. The transplantation of nuclei between two species of Xenopus. Dev. Biol. 5, 68–83 (1962)."),[23](#ref-CR23 "Gurdon, J. B. Adult frogs derived from the nuclei of single somatic cells. Dev. Biol. 4, 256–273 (1962)."),[24](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR24 "Gurdon, J. B. Multiple genetically identical frogs. J. Hered. 53, 5–9 (1962).") Therefore, the SCNT experiments revealed that genetic information was preserved during differentiation, whereas phenotypic diversity of somatic cells was likely achieved by reversible epigenetic mechanisms. What kind of epigenetic mechanisms could enable such elaborate yet reversible phenotypic diversity? Among the many layers of epigenetic regulation known today, DNA methylation is a prominent example of stable, yet reversible epigenetic memory acquired along the course of cell fate specification.[26](#ref-CR26 "Jeltsch, A. & Jurkowska, R. Z. New concepts in DNA methylation. Trends Biochem. Sci. 39, 310–318 (2014)."),[27](#ref-CR27 "Riggs, A. D. X inactivation, differentiation, and DNA methylation. Cytogenet. Cell Genet. 14, 9–25 (1975)."),[28](#ref-CR28 "Robertson, K. D. & Wolffe, A. P. DNA methylation in health and disease. Nat. Rev. Genet. 1, 11–19 (2000)."),[29](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR29 "Schubeler, D. Function and information content of DNA methylation. Nature 517, 321–326 (2015).") For a historical review of discovering DNA methylation as a central mechanism of gene expression regulation and maintenance over mitotic divisions, the readers are referred to Tompkins, 2022.[14](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR14 "Tompkins, J. D. Discovering DNA methylation, the history and future of the writing on DNA. J. Hist. Biol. 55, 865–887 (2022).")

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41392-024-01809-0/MediaObjects/41392_2024_1809_Fig1_HTML.png)

Development of the induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC) technology. **a** A timeline of key breakthroughs related to the iPSC technology. **b** (Top) Somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT) experiments were pioneered by John Gurdon in the African clawed frog. Gurdon demonstrated that somatic cells retained all the genetic information necessary to give rise to a germline-competent organism. Successful SCNT in mammals was demonstrated by Keith Campbell, Ian Wilmut, and colleagues who cloned Dolly the sheep. (Bottom) Masako Tada and colleagues demonstrated that pluripotency can also be achieved by fusing a somatic cell with an embryonic stem cell, leading to the formation of a hybrid tetraploid cell. 4N, tetraploid. **c** The groundbreaking experiments of fibroblast reprogramming to pluripotency were pioneered by Kazutoshi Takahashi and Shinya Yamanaka. The researchers selected 24 factors as candidates for reprogramming and delivered these factors into mouse fibroblasts in various combinations by retroviral transduction. Eventually, Takahashi and Yamanaka identified a combination of 4 reprogramming factors—Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, and Myc—that was sufficient to reprogram mouse fibroblasts into embryonic stem cell-like pluripotent cells, known as iPSCs. Subsequently, Yamanaka and James Thomson independently reprogrammed human fibroblasts into iPSCs in 2007

In 1981, British biologists Martin Evans (b. 1941) and Matthew Kaufman (1942–2013) as well as the American biologist Gail Martin (b. 1944) isolated mouse embryonic stem cells (ESCs) that would serve as a reference point for subsequent somatic cell reprogramming experiments.[30](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR30 "Evans, M. J. & Kaufman, M. H. Establishment in culture of pluripotential cells from mouse embryos. Nature 292, 154–156 (1981)."),[31](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR31 "Martin, G. R. Isolation of a pluripotent cell line from early mouse embryos cultured in medium conditioned by teratocarcinoma stem cells. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 78, 7634–7638 (1981).") Human ESCs were isolated by the American developmental biologist James Thomson (b. 1958) and colleagues in 1998.[32](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR32 "Thomson, J. A. et al. Embryonic stem cell lines derived from human blastocysts. Science 282, 1145–1147 (1998).") Cell fusion experiments of mouse[33](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR33 "Tada, M., Takahama, Y., Abe, K., Nakatsuji, N. & Tada, T. Nuclear reprogramming of somatic cells by in vitro hybridization with ES cells. Curr. Biol. 11, 1553–1558 (2001).") and human[34](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR34 "Cowan, C. A., Atienza, J., Melton, D. A. & Eggan, K. Nuclear reprogramming of somatic cells after fusion with human embryonic stem cells. Science 309, 1369–1373 (2005).") ESCs with somatic cells revealed the capacity of the resulting heterokaryon for reprogramming to pluripotency, thus reaffirming the notion of cellular plasticity and somatic cell fate reversibility observed by Gurdon (Fig. [1b](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#Fig1)). Transdifferentiation experiments by ectopic expression of transcriptions factors further revealed the importance of transcription factors in establishing cell fate; for example, overexpression of the C/EBPα/β transcription factors was found to promote B cell reprogramming into macrophages.[35](#ref-CR35 "Davis, R. L., Weintraub, H. & Lassar, A. B. Expression of a single transfected cDNA converts fibroblasts to myoblasts. Cell 51, 987–1000 (1987)."),[36](#ref-CR36 "Halder, G., Callaerts, P. & Gehring, W. J. Induction of ectopic eyes by targeted expression of the eyeless gene in drosophila. Science 267, 1788–1792 (1995)."),[37](#ref-CR37 "Kulessa, H., Frampton, J. & Graf, T. Gata-1 reprograms Avian Myelomonocytic cell-lines into Eosinophils, Thromboblasts, and Erythroblasts. Gene Dev. 9, 1250–1262 (1995)."),[38](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR38 "Xie, H., Ye, M., Feng, R. & Graf, T. Stepwise reprogramming of B cells into macrophages. Cell 117, 663–676 (2004).") With ESCs as a reference point for features of pluripotency and an emerging understanding of how transcription factors orchestrated gene expression, the Japanese stem cell biologist Shinya Yamanaka (b. 1962) together with his postdoctoral fellow Kazutoshi Takahashi designed a series of somatic cell reprogramming experiments that would lead to the breakthrough development of mouse iPSCs in 2006 (Fig. [1c](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#Fig1)).[4](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR4 "Takahashi, K. & Yamanaka, S. Induction of pluripotent stem cells from mouse embryonic and adult fibroblast cultures by defined factors. Cell 126, 663–676 (2006).") Aiming to induce pluripotency in mouse embryonic fibroblasts (MEFs), Takahashi and Yamanaka selected 24 potential reprogramming factors that included transcription factors known to be important for the ESC state and other effectors. The reprogramming factors were cloned into retroviral vectors for MEF transduction, whereas MEFs were engineered to carry β-galactosidase and neomycin resistance encoding genes under a pluripotency-specific promoter of the *Fbxo15* gene. Screening different combination of the 24 reprogramming factors, Takahashi and Yamanaka narrowed down the list to four transcription factors that were sufficient to induce pluripotency in MEFs: Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, and Myc (together known as OSKM or Yamanaka factors).[4](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR4 "Takahashi, K. & Yamanaka, S. Induction of pluripotent stem cells from mouse embryonic and adult fibroblast cultures by defined factors. Cell 126, 663–676 (2006).") Remarkably, these mouse iPSCs resembled the biological potency, gene expression, and the epigenetic landscape of ESCs.[39](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR39 "Wernig, M. et al. In vitro reprogramming of fibroblasts into a pluripotent ES-cell-like state. Nature 448, 318–324 (2007).") A year later, Yamanaka and Thomson independently demonstrated that human fibroblasts could also be reprogrammed into iPSCs; Yamanaka used the same OSKM factors, whereas Thomson used OCT4, SOX2, NANOG, and LIN28.[3](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR3 "Takahashi, K. et al. Induction of pluripotent stem cells from adult human fibroblasts by defined factors. Cell 131, 861–872 (2007)."),[5](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR5 "Yu, J. et al. Induced pluripotent stem cell lines derived from human somatic cells. Science 318, 1917–1920 (2007).") These combinations of reprogramming factors remain widely used today, whereas Gurdon and Yamanaka were awarded the 2012 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for their discoveries. Since 2007, various modifications to the original cocktail of reprogramming factors have been developed. For example, small-molecule assisted somatic cell reprogramming was first reported in 2008,[40](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR40 "Huangfu, D. W. et al. Induction of pluripotent stem cells by defined factors is greatly improved by small-molecule compounds. Nat. Biotechnol. 26, 795–797 (2008)."),[41](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR41 "Huangfu, D. et al. Induction of pluripotent stem cells from primary human fibroblasts with only Oct4 and Sox2. Nat. Biotechnol. 26, 1269–1275 (2008).") whereas fully chemical reprogramming of murine fibroblasts using seven small-molecule compounds was achieved in 2013.[42](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR42 "Hou, P. et al. Pluripotent stem cells induced from mouse somatic cells by small-molecule compounds. Science 341, 651–654 (2013).")

## Molecular mechanisms of somatic cell reprogramming to iPSCs

When pluripotent stem cells undergo differentiation into somatic cells, they acquire epigenetic memory and undergo global changes to their chromatin conformation, resulting in inactivation of pluripotency-specific genes and activation of somatic cell-specific genes.[43](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR43 "Zhu, J. et al. Genome-wide chromatin state transitions associated with developmental and environmental cues. Cell 152, 642–654 (2013).") Reprogramming of somatic cells back to the pluripotency state involves the erasure of many of these somatic cell signatures; therefore, induction of pluripotency has been proposed to partially resemble the a sequence of developmental events in reverse.[6](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR6 "Takahashi, K. & Yamanaka, S. A decade of transcription factor-mediated reprogramming to pluripotency. Nat. Rev. Mol. Cell Biol. 17, 183–193 (2016)."),[44](#ref-CR44 "Apostolou, E. & Hochedlinger, K. Chromatin dynamics during cellular reprogramming. Nature 502, 462–471 (2013)."),[45](#ref-CR45 "Apostolou, E. & Stadtfeld, M. Cellular trajectories and molecular mechanisms of iPSC reprogramming. Curr. Opin. Genet. Dev. 52, 77–85 (2018)."),[46](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR46 "Cacchiarelli, D. et al. Integrative analyses of human reprogramming reveal dynamic nature of induced pluripotency. Cell 162, 412–424 (2015).") Broadly, reprogramming occurs in two phases, early and late. During the early phase, somatic genes are silenced, whereas early pluripotency-associated genes are activated; during the late phase, late pluripotency-associated genes are activated. Early events of reprogramming are largely stochastic, presumably owing to the inefficient access of closed chromatin by OSKM and other transcription factors, whereas late events appear to be more deterministic.[6](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR6 "Takahashi, K. & Yamanaka, S. A decade of transcription factor-mediated reprogramming to pluripotency. Nat. Rev. Mol. Cell Biol. 17, 183–193 (2016).") Universal aspects of reprogramming, such as two transcriptional waves, are accompanied by somatic cell type-specific reprogramming trajectories and transient events.[47](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR47 "Nefzger, C. M. et al. Cell type of origin dictates the route to pluripotency. Cell Rep. 21, 2649–2660 (2017).") Overall, reprogramming entails profound remodeling of the chromatin structure and the epigenome as well as changes to almost every aspect of cell biology, including metabolism, cell signaling, intracellular transport, proteostasis, and others.[48](#ref-CR48 "Borkent, M. et al. A serial shRNA screen for roadblocks to reprogramming identifies the protein modifier SUMO2. Stem Cell Rep. 6, 704–716 (2016)."),[49](#ref-CR49 "Buckley, S. M. et al. Regulation of Pluripotency and cellular reprogramming by the ubiquitin-proteasome system. Cell Stem Cell 11, 783–798 (2012)."),[50](#ref-CR50 "Qin, H. et al. Systematic identification of barriers to human iPSC generation. Cell 158, 449–461 (2014)."),[51](#ref-CR51 "Simic, M. S. et al. Transient activation of the UPR(ER) is an essential step in the acquisition of pluripotency during reprogramming. Sci. Adv. 5, eaaw0025 (2019)."),[52](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR52 "Wu, Y. et al. Phospholipid remodeling is critical for stem cell pluripotency by facilitating mesenchymal-to-epithelial transition. Sci. Adv. 5, eaax7525 (2019).") Given that iPSCs are most often derived from fibroblasts, mesenchymal-to-epithelial transition (MET) is another critical event that occurs during reprogramming.[53](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR53 "Pei, D. Q., Shu, X. D., Gassama-Diagne, A. & Thiery, J. P. Mesenchymal-epithelial transition in development and reprogramming. Nat. Cell Biol. 21, 44–53 (2019).")

Uncovering the molecular mechanisms of iPSC induction facilitates the development of novel reprogramming approaches and reveals the underlying principles of cell fate transitions and cell fate determination. This knowledge can subsequently be used to design rational strategies for iPSC differentiation towards the desired cell types in an efficient manner. In this section, we focus on the roles of transcription factors as well as chromatin and DNA methylation dynamics in reprogramming.

### Transcription factors

OSKM and other transcription factors orchestrate somatic cell reprogramming to pluripotency.[54](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR54 "Soufi, A., Donahue, G. & Zaret, K. S. Facilitators and impediments of the pluripotency reprogramming factors’ initial engagement with the genome. Cell 151, 994–1004 (2012)."),[55](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR55 "Soufi, A. et al. Pioneer transcription factors target partial DNA Motifs on nucleosomes to initiate reprogramming. Cell 161, 555–568 (2015).") Through concerted action, OSKM expel somatic cell-specific transcription factors from somatic enhancers and activate pluripotency enhancers; silencing of somatic cell-specific enhancers is initiated early in reprogramming, whereas activation of pluripotency-specific enhancers occurs later in reprogramming.[56](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR56 "Chen, J. et al. Hierarchical Oct4 binding in concert with primed epigenetic rearrangements during somatic cell reprogramming. Cell Rep. 14, 1540–1554 (2016)."),[57](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR57 "Chronis, C. et al. Cooperative binding of transcription factors orchestrates reprogramming. Cell 168, 442–459.e420 (2017).") Notably, the chromatin and DNA methylation landscape is restrictive early in reprogramming, requiring pioneering activity of the OSKM factors to access closed chromatin and initiate gene expression.[58](/articles/s41392-024-01809-0#ref-CR58

enriched_points.expanded_content: Stem cell biology is advancing rapidly, integrating new technologies and concepts for improved understanding of development, regeneration, and disease mechanisms.
Key mechanisms include cellular reprogramming, differentiation pathways, and gene regulation, which are crucial for harnessing stem cells for regenerative therapies and disease modeling.

Clinical applications of stem cell research include treatment for degenerative diseases, tissue engineering, and personalized medicine approaches, revolutionizing healthcare and regenerative therapies.
